1: sēmeion mega ōphthē en tō ouranō: "a great sign appeared in heaven." Sēmeion (sign, portent) signals that what follows is symbolic vision, not literal astronomy. Revelation has used sēmeion for its major symbolic visions (Revelation 12:1, 3; Revelation 15:1). The woman clothed with sun, moon under her feet, and twelve stars: the imagery of Genesis 37:9 (Joseph's dream — sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing to him) now applied to the covenant people as a cosmic figure. The twelve stars = twelve tribes of Israel, though in Revelation's typological reading, the woman is simultaneously OT Israel (through whom the Messiah came) and the NT church (the ongoing covenant community).
2: ōdinousan kai basanizomenēn tekein: in labor pains (ōdinousan) and torment (basanizomenēn) to give birth. The birth-pang imagery activates Isaiah 66:7-8 (before she was in labor she gave birth; before her pain came upon her she delivered a son — the miraculous Zion-birth) and Micah 5:2-3 (she who is in labor will give birth, and the rest of his brothers will return). These OT texts describe Zion's eschatological birth of the Messiah. The pain signals that the birth is historical, not triumphalist — the covenant community suffers in bringing forth the Messiah.
3: drakon megas pyrros: a great red dragon. Drakon in the LXX regularly translates Hebrew תַּנִּין (tannin), the sea-monster of primordial chaos (Psalms 74:13-14; Isaiah 27:1; Revelation 51:9; Ezekiel 29:3). The seven heads and ten horns combine features from Daniel's four beasts (Daniel 7:3-7 — lion, bear, leopard, terrible beast with ten horns) into one super-beast. The seven diadems (hepta diademata) are royal crowns — the dragon claims the sovereignty that belongs to God alone. The color red (pyrros) signals bloodshed and violence.
4: hē oura autou syrei to triton tōn asterōn tou ouranou: his tail drags (syrei, drags along forcibly) a third of the stars and hurls them to earth. Stars in Revelation and Daniel can represent angelic beings (Daniel 8:10; Job 38:7); the dragon's expulsion of a third of the heavenly host is the mythological background for the origin of demonic beings — the fallen angels who follow Satan's rebellion. The dragon's positioning (hestēken, standing, present tense: stationed himself) before the woman is the posture of the predator waiting to devour the newborn — echoing Pharaoh's infanticide (Exodus 1:15-22) and Herod's (Matthew 2:16).
5:
arsen: the masculine form emphasizing the child's gender identity as the Messiah-king. Ho mellōn poimainein panta ta ethnē en rhabdō sidēra (who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron) — the direct citation of Psalms 2:9, the royal enthronement psalm that speaks of the anointed king's dominion. The male child's destiny is established by psalm before he acts.

hērpasthē pros ton theon: hērpasthē (aorist passive of harpazō) — was snatched up, caught away. The Ascension compressed into one violent verb: Christ's exaltation is not a peaceful gradual lifting but an eschatological rescue from the dragon's reach. The rapture of the child to God's throne (pros ton theon kai pros ton thronon autou) bypasses the entire earthly ministry to focus on the Incarnation-Death-Resurrection-Ascension as one act of deliverance.
6:
eis tēn erēmon: the wilderness — the Exodus typology. The protection of the woman in the wilderness echoes God's protection of Israel in the Sinai wilderness (Exodus 19:4; Deuteronomy 32:10-12; Isaiah 40:3; Jeremiah 31:2). The erēmos in Revelation is not desolation but the place of divine preparation and preservation. Ετοίμασεν (prepared) — God prepared the place, not circumstance: the community's protection is by divine provision, not human ingenuity.

chilias diakosioi hexēkonta hēmerai: 1,260 days = 42 months = 3½ years = "a time, times, and half a time" (Daniel 7:25; Revelation 12:7). This figure from Daniel represents the duration of the Antichrist's oppression — the eschatological half-week (half of Daniel's symbolic seven). In Revelation it frames the entire period between Christ's ascension and parousia as simultaneously the time of the beast's apparent triumph and the community's protected tribulation.
7: Michaēl kai hoi angeloi autou: Michael and his angels. Michaēl = Hebrew מִיכָאֵל (Mika'el, "who is like God?") — the archangel who is Israel's guardian prince in Daniel 10:13, 21; Revelation 12:1, and in Jude 9 fights over Moses' body. The war in heaven (polemos en tō ouranō) is not a cosmological event prior to creation but a consequence of the Messiah's ascension (Revelation 12:5): Christ's exaltation to God's throne triggers the expulsion of the accuser from the heavenly court. The timing is theologically precise: the war follows the coronation.
8: ouk ischysen: he was not strong enough — a flat, unqualified statement of the dragon's defeat. Ouk ischysen (imperfect of ischyō: he kept not being strong enough, he proved insufficient). Oude topos heurethē autois eti en tō ouranō: no place (topos) was found for them any longer in heaven. The denial of topos is significant: in Job 1-2, Satan had ongoing access to the heavenly court. The Resurrection-Ascension removes this access permanently — the accuser is evicted from the divine courtroom where he prosecuted the saints.
9: ho ophis ho archaios: the ancient (archaios = original, from the beginning) serpent — explicitly identifying the dragon with the Eden serpent of Gen 3. Revelation's interpretive key: the cosmic dragon behind world history is the same creature that deceived Eve. The three titles given: diabolos (devil = slanderer, false accuser), Satanas (from Hebrew שָׂטָן, satan = adversary, accuser), ho planōn tēn oikoumenēn holēn (the one who deceives the whole inhabited world — present participle: ongoing deception). The Greek diabolos and Hebrew satan are nearly synonymous: both mean adversarial accuser. Revelation gives both names to mark continuity across both testaments.
10: katēgōr tōn adelphōn hēmōn: the accuser (katēgōr) of our brothers. Katēgōr is a rare form (also katēgoros elsewhere), possibly a Greek loan from Aramaic katēgora. The picture is legal: the dragon has been prosecuting the saints before God's court day and night (hēmeras kai nyktos). This matches the Job 1-2 and Zechariah 3:1 pattern where the adversary brings charges against God's people before the divine throne. The defeat announced: eblēthē (was thrown down, aorist: a completed expulsion) — the accuser has been permanently removed from his prosecutorial role.
11: enikēsan auton dia to haima tou Arniou kai dia ton logon tēs martyrias autōn: they conquered him by two instruments — the blood of the Lamb (objective) and the word of their testimony (subjective). The Lamb's blood answers every charge the accuser can bring; the saints' faithful testimony (even unto death) corroborates the verdict. Ouk ēgapēsan tēn psychēn autōn achri thanatou: they did not love their psychē (life-force, not just the immortal soul) to the point of death — the paradox of martyrdom: by losing their lives they win the war. The ψυχή they sacrifice is precisely what the dragon attacks.
12: euphrainesthē hoi ouranoi: let the heavens rejoice — plural imperative addressed to the heavenly realm. The doubled response: rejoicing for heaven, ouai (woe) for the earth and sea. Ouai is the lament/woe cry used in prophetic oracles (Isaiah 5:8-23; Amos 5:18; Matt 23). The dragon's expulsion means heaven's peace but earth's intensified conflict: katebē ho diabolos pros hymas echōn thymon megan eidōs hoti oligon kairon echei (knowing he has a short time). Thymos (rage, fury — also the word for the wrath of God in the bowl judgments) now characterizes the defeated dragon: fury born from desperation, not power.
13: ote eiden ho drakōn hoti eblēthē eis tēn gēn: when the dragon saw (eidon) that he was thrown to the earth, he pursued (ediōxen) the woman. The dragon's response to defeat is not repentance but intensified persecution — the knowledge of defeat does not produce humility but rage. The pursuit of the woman on earth = the historical persecution of the covenant community after Christ's ascension. The dragon can no longer access the heavenly court so redirects his hostility to earth.
14: hai dyo pteryx tou aetou tou megalou: the two wings of the great eagle — directly quoting the Exodus deliverance language of Exodus 19:4 (I bore you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself) and Deuteronomy 32:11 (like an eagle that stirs up its nest, that hovers over its young, spreading out its wings, catching them, bearing them on its pinions). The eagle-wings = divine protection and transport through tribulation. The wilderness period (time, times, and half a time = 3½ years = the same duration as Revelation 12:6) repeats: the protected period is the entire church age, not a literal 3½ years.
15: ebalen ho ophis ek tou stomatos autou opisō tēs gynaikos hydōr hōs potamon: the serpent poured water (hydōr) like a river from his mouth after the woman. The flood of persecution — the river (potamos) as weapon of destruction echoes Psalms 18:4 (the torrents of destruction assailed me), Psalms 124:4-5 (then the flood would have swept us away), and Isaiah 43:2 (when you pass through the rivers, they shall not overwhelm you). The serpent's flood = the overwhelming power of empire-backed persecution attempting to sweep away the covenant community.
16: ēnoixen hē gē to stoma autēs kai katepien ton potamon: the earth opened its mouth and swallowed the river. Katepien (swallowed) — the same verb used in Numbers 16:30, 32, 34 for the earth swallowing Korah's company who rebelled against Moses. The earth, which could be a venue for persecution, here becomes the protector of the covenant community — creation itself defends the people of God. The subversion of the serpent's plot by the creature he had corrupted carries ironic theological weight.
17: to loipon tou spermatos autēs: the rest of her offspring (sperma = seed). The seed (sperma) language deliberately echoes Genesis 3:15 (I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her seed). Revelation identifies the church as the woman's seed — those who keep the commandments of God and hold the testimony of Jesus (echontōn tēn martyrian Iēsou). The dragon's war against the rest of the seed is the ongoing conflict of Genesis 3:15, now understood as the persecution of Christ's followers.
